title: Module 0 · AI Agent Fundamentals
objective: Understand what an AI agent is, how it processes a task, and how to explain it without jargon.

quiz.description: Answer these questions to validate you understand the core idea of an agent.

questions.prompt: What does an AI agent do more precisely?
options: It guesses without context and outputs magic
It receives a goal, reasons, and acts with allowed tools
questions.explanation: The core idea is goal + context + reasoning + allowed actions.

questions.prompt: What is the best starting point according to this course?
options: Understand the problem and plan first
questions.explanation: The template emphasizes understanding and planning before coding to reduce rework.

sections.heading: What is an AI agent?
paragraphs: An AI agent receives a goal, thinks through steps to solve it, and executes allowed actions.
It is not magic: results improve when you provide clear instructions, context, and quality criteria.

sections.heading: Agent anatomy (diagram)
paragraphs: Use this 2D map to understand the minimal pieces of an agent and how they connect.
sections.diagram:
flowchart TD
  U[User] --> I[Instructions]
  I --> C[Context]
  C --> R[Reasoning]
  R --> T[Allowed tools]
  T --> O[Output]
  O --> V[Validation]

sections.heading: Practical work cycle
paragraphs: Good work starts with understanding and planning, not code first.
This cycle helps you avoid rework and late-stage hard-to-debug failures.
sections.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Understand problem] --> B[Plan]
  B --> C[Implement small]
  C --> D[Test]
  D --> E[Review]
  E --> F{Goal met?}
  F -- No --> B
  F -- Yes --> G[Deliver]

title: Module 1 · Skills and Responsibilities
objective: Separate roles so planning, development, and QA are not mixed.

quiz.description: Check whether you can distinguish responsibilities between skills.

questions.prompt: Which skill focuses on defining coverage and critical cases?
questions.explanation: qa-strategy defines validation coverage across happy path, edge cases, and negatives.

questions.prompt: Which is a clear anti-pattern?
questions.explanation: Starting with code breaks the workflow and usually increases risk and rework.

sections.heading: Why separate responsibilities
paragraphs: When one flow mixes everything (plan, code, testing, review), errors and rework increase.
Using role-focused skills improves focus, validation quality, and risk detection.
bullets: qa-strategy: defines coverage and critical cases

sections.heading: Skill interaction (diagram)
paragraphs: This 2D map shows what each role outputs and how the next role consumes it.
sections.diagram:
flowchart TB
  A[planner] -->|Plan + risks| B[qa-strategy]
  B -->|Coverage + cases| C[dev]
  C -->|Change + tests| D[reviewer]
  D -->|Findings| E[qa-regression]
  E -->|Final status| F[release]

sections.heading: Anti-patterns to avoid
paragraphs: These common mistakes help you quickly detect broken workflow behavior.
bullets: Starting with code and no plan

sections.heading: Operational checklist (diagram)
paragraphs: Use this compact flow as a daily execution checklist.
sections.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Clear goal] --> B[Short plan]
  B --> C[QA cases]
  C --> D[Implementation]
  D --> E[Tests]
  E --> F[Review]
  F --> G[Document and deliver]

title: Module 2 · Full Workflow
objective: Walk through the template workflow with real examples.

quiz.description: Validate that you can follow the end-to-end flow without losing traceability.

questions.prompt: Which phase comes right after planner in the official flow?
questions.explanation: qa-strategy immediately follows planner to define what to validate before coding.

questions.prompt: What should dev produce so reviewer can evaluate effectively?
questions.explanation: Reviewer needs concrete evidence: implementation plus tests showing behavior.

sections.heading: End-to-end execution map
paragraphs: This module connects all parts so you see how an idea becomes a reliable delivery.
Traceability is key: each phase produces output consumed by the next one.
sections.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Requirement] --> B[planner]
  B --> C[qa-strategy]
  C --> D[dev]
  D --> E[qa-functional / qa-api]
  E --> F[reviewer]
  F --> G[qa-regression]
  G --> H[Release candidate]

sections.heading: Interactive workflow simulator
paragraphs: Use this simulator to walk the process in practice mode. Each step shows actor, action, and output.

simulator.title: Simulator: from idea to delivery
simulator.description: Move step by step to understand handoffs between roles.

steps.action: Clarifies goal, assumptions, and key risks.
steps.output: Validated plan with scope and priorities.

steps.action: Defines coverage: happy path, negatives, and edge cases.
Checks that new changes do not break existing behavior.

sections.heading: Workflow health indicators
paragraphs: These indicators help you know whether the workflow is working in practice.
bullets: Tests pass before closing an iteration
Every change has reason and validation evidence

title: Module 3 · Skill Lab I (00, 10, 20)
objective: Understand and apply 00-project-profile, 10-plan-first, and 20-safe-changes in detail.

quiz.description: Check whether you can distinguish the operational purpose of each foundation skill.

questions.prompt: What is the main goal of 00-project-profile?
options: Store stack, commands, and constraints context shared by other skills
questions.explanation: 00-project-profile is a shared source of truth for stack, commands, and guardrails.

questions.prompt: According to 10-plan-first, what should exist before editing code?
options: A concrete plan with assumptions, risks, files, and verification
questions.explanation: 10-plan-first enforces a planning output format before implementation.

questions.prompt: Which anti-pattern does 20-safe-changes avoid?
options: Changing many unrelated files in one shot
questions.explanation: 20-safe-changes promotes small scoped diffs with checkpoints to lower risk.

sections.heading: Skill 00 · project-profile (overview)
paragraphs: This skill defines baseline project context: identity, stack, commands, constraints, and definition of done.
Think of it as minimal configuration that prevents inconsistent decisions across agents and workflow stages.
bullets: Identity: project name, repo type, and domain
Commands: install, lint, unit/e2e tests, build
Constraints: dangerous actions and safe zones

sections.heading: Skill 00 · how to fill it correctly
paragraphs: Keep it short and actionable. Do not write essays, write operational decisions.
If this file is ambiguous, all other skills lose precision in execution and validation.
bullets: Use commands that really work in the repository
Declare what should never be changed without asking
Include secrets policy and measurable DoD
Update this skill whenever stack changes

sections.heading: Skill 10 · plan-first in action
paragraphs: This skill enforces a plan template before coding: goal, assumptions, plan, files, risks, and checklist.
Its practical value is auditable reasoning and fewer improvisation mistakes.
sections.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Goal] --> B[Assumptions]
  B --> C[Plan <= 7 steps]
  C --> D[Files to touch]
  D --> E[Top 3 risks]
  E --> F[Verification checklist]

sections.heading: Skill 20 · safe-changes in action
paragraphs: Once the plan exists, this skill controls execution quality to avoid breaking the system.
Golden rule: small changes, single intent, frequent validation.
bullets: Always deliver: what changed, how to verify, rollback notes

title: Module 4 · Skill Lab II (30, 40)
objective: Apply structured review and testing strategy to close the quality loop.

quiz.description: Close the lab by validating code review and testing strategy criteria.

questions.prompt: What output does 30-code-review require?
questions.explanation: Skill 30 requires prioritized findings and explicit open questions.

questions.prompt: In 40-tests, what minimum coverage is required?
options: Happy path and at least one key negative case
questions.explanation: 40-tests asks for baseline risk coverage with happy path + key negative case.

questions.prompt: Why are 30 + 40 considered quality closure?
options: Because they validate both implementation quality and behavior evidence
questions.explanation: Review plus testing give confidence in correctness, security, and maintainability before release.

sections.heading: Skill 30 · code-review checklist
paragraphs: This skill standardizes review across 4 axes: correctness, security, maintainability, and tests.
Its role is not code editing; it is risk detection and quality elevation.
bullets: Security: input validation and secrets handling
Tests: changed behavior coverage and flaky risk

sections.heading: Skill 40 · testing strategy
paragraphs: This skill forces conscious test-level selection: unit, integration, or e2e.
It also requires clarity on what is automated versus manual and why.
sections.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Change identified] --> B{Right level?}
  B -->|Pure logic| C[Unit]
  B -->|DB/API boundaries| D[Integration]
  B -->|User flow| E[E2E]
  C --> F[Happy + negative]
  D --> F
  E --> F
  F --> G[Flaky risk + mitigation]

sections.heading: Integrating 30 + 40 in the workflow
paragraphs: In this template, technical and behavioral validation after implementation is not optional.
30 and 40 provide a shared language to decide if work is truly ready to move forward.
bullets: No test evidence means no confidence
Critical uncertainties are captured as explicit questions

title: Module 5 · Meta Session: how chat interprets .md files
objective: Understand how repository markdown files influence decisions, execution order, and agent behavior.

quiz.description: Validate your understanding of markdown file hierarchy and workflow roles.

questions.prompt: If documentation conflicts, which file wins according to AGENTS.md?
options: AGENTS.md as the main source of truth
questions.explanation: AGENTS.md explicitly defines priority and acts as the operational source of truth.

questions.prompt: What is the role of a SKILL.md file?
options: Provide specialized guidance for a task type
questions.explanation: A skill encapsulates practical best practices for specific scenarios (planning, testing, review, etc.).

questions.prompt: How does chat interact with these .md files in practice?
options: It reads them as rules/context and decides actions by priority and scope
It ignores them if source code exists
It only uses them at task completion
questions.explanation: The chat uses markdown guidance as normative context for planning, execution, and validation.

sections.heading: Map of .md files in this repository
paragraphs: Not all markdown files serve the same purpose. Some explain concepts, others govern agent behavior.
In this template, AGENTS.md, AI_WORKFLOW.md, SYSTEM_OVERVIEW.md, and SKILL.md are the core process layer.

sections.heading: Hierarchy and conflict resolution
paragraphs: When two instructions conflict, the agent should not improvise: it applies explicit priority order.
This avoids arbitrary behavior and makes decision paths auditable.
sections.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[System/Developer rules] --> B[AGENTS.md]
  B --> C[.agents/*.md]
  C --> D[.codex/agents/*.toml]
  D --> E[Task execution]
  E --> F[Validation + reporting]

sections.heading: How chat uses SKILL.md live
paragraphs: Before acting, the agent identifies relevant skills and loads them.
Then it adapts plan and output format based on that skill (e.g., plan-first, tests, safe-changes).
bullets: Detect relevant skill by task intent

sections.heading: Practical case: from prompt to action
paragraphs: When you ask “continue building the course”, chat does more than write text: it first decides applicable rules.
With that context, it plans, edits files, runs tests, and reports evidence.
sections.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant C as Chat
  participant M as Markdown rules
  participant F as Files/Tests
  U->>C: Request
  C->>M: Load AGENTS + SKILL
  M-->>C: Rules and workflow
  C->>F: Edit and validate
  F-->>C: Results
  C-->>U: Summary + verification

title: Module 6 · .agents layer: roles and skills
objective: Understand .agents as the conceptual workflow layer and how agents, mapping, and skills connect.

quiz.description: Validate your understanding of .agents purpose and relationship with the official flow.

questions.prompt: What does the .agents folder primarily represent?
questions.explanation: .agents describes the operational model: who does what and when.

questions.prompt: What is AGENT_MAPPING.md used for?
options: Map conceptual agents to runtime execution configuration
questions.explanation: The mapping bridges conceptual intent and runtime layer.

questions.prompt: Which statement about SKILL.md is correct?
options: It defines specialized protocols for concrete task types
It does not affect agent decisions
questions.explanation: Skills guide practical execution through guardrails and checklists.

sections.heading: What lives inside .agents
paragraphs: The .agents folder defines the conceptual map: roles, boundaries, and responsibilities across phases.
It includes files like planner.agent.md, dev.agent.md, qa-*.agent.md, reviewer.agent.md, and meta.agent.md.

sections.heading: Role sequence in .agents
paragraphs: Roles are not decorative: ordering prevents responsibility mixing and minimizes rework.
sections.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[planner.agent.md] --> B[qa-strategy.agent.md]
  B --> C[dev.agent.md]
  C --> D[qa-functional / qa-api]
  D --> E[reviewer.agent.md]
  E --> F[qa-regression.agent.md]
  F --> G[meta/security optional]

sections.heading: Core skills in .agents/skills
paragraphs: Each skill contributes a concrete workflow piece, such as plan-first or safe-changes.
Think of skills as reusable micro-protocols that an agent loads by task context.

title: Module 7 · .codex layer: runtime configuration
objective: Distinguish runtime layer (.codex/agents/*.toml) and how it materializes conceptual design.

quiz.description: Check that you can separate conceptual intent from execution configuration.

questions.prompt: What kind of files live in .codex/agents?
questions.explanation: The .codex/agents folder contains runtime configuration per agent.

questions.prompt: builder.toml is mainly associated with:
questions.explanation: builder reflects implementation/runtime behavior.

questions.prompt: If you want to change role responsibilities, where should you start?
questions.explanation: Intent should be defined in .agents first and then reflected in .codex.

sections.heading: What .codex solves
paragraphs: While .agents says what should happen, .codex defines how that runs at runtime.
It is the operational translation of conceptual design into executable configuration.

sections.heading: Bridge between layers
paragraphs: A robust architecture separates intent from runtime to simplify maintenance and evolution.
sections.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[.agents/*.md (intent)] --> B[AGENT_MAPPING.md]
  B --> C[.codex/agents/*.toml (runtime)]
  C --> D[Observable behavior]
  D --> E[Validation and tests]

sections.heading: Best practices when editing .codex
paragraphs: Avoid runtime edits without conceptual impact validation first.
Any change here should include traceability and verification evidence.

title: Module 8 · Root markdowns and workflow governance
objective: Understand how root markdown files coordinate rules, safety, and project traceability.

quiz.description: Validate mastery of AGENTS.md, AGENTS_CHANGELOG.md, and other root markdown roles.

questions.prompt: Which document wins in instruction conflicts?
questions.explanation: AGENTS.md explicitly defines decision hierarchy.

questions.prompt: What is AGENTS_CHANGELOG.md used for?
questions.explanation: It provides operational traceability of key workflow changes.

questions.prompt: What does SECURITY.md contribute in this architecture?
questions.explanation: SECURITY.md defines risk-reduction practices and unsafe-pattern guardrails.

sections.heading: Root markdown governance map
paragraphs: The repository root concentrates governance documents for the full system.
Reading them in the right order accelerates onboarding and prevents contradictory decisions.
bullets: AI_WORKFLOW.md and SYSTEM_OVERVIEW.md: functional guide and system map
README.md and SECURITY.md: technical entry point and policy guardrails

sections.heading: Recommended incremental path for new users
paragraphs: This sequence gives progressive context without overwhelming first-time readers.
sections.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[README.md] --> B[SYSTEM_OVERVIEW.md]
  B --> C[AI_WORKFLOW.md]
  C --> D[AGENTS.md]
  D --> E[.agents + skills]
  E --> F[.codex runtime]
  F --> G[AGENTS_CHANGELOG.md]

sections.heading: Governance checklist for future iterations
paragraphs: As the project grows, documentation discipline reduces knowledge debt.
Use this checklist on every workflow release.
bullets: Update source-of-truth markdowns if rules change
